title: Franchise Accounting in QuickBooks: One File Per Unit or Classes? The Decision Framework
excerpt: One QuickBooks file with classes, or a company file per franchise unit? A criteria-by-criteria decision framework for multi-unit owners.
author: FinBoard Team
coverAlt: Franchise Accounting in QuickBooks: One File Per Unit or Classes? The Decision Framework

@graph.description: A decision framework for multi-unit franchise accounting in QuickBooks Online: one company file per unit versus classes/locations in a single file. Covers legal entity separation, separate bank accounts, franchisor reporting mandates, royalty and ad-fund calculation, lender and landlord reporting, shared-cost allocation, the hybrid file-per-entity-plus-consolidation-layer model, and a migration path from classes to separate entities.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a separate QuickBooks company for each franchise location?

acceptedAnswer.text: If each location is a separate legal entity (its own LLC or corporation), yes. Each entity needs its own QuickBooks company file to keep legal, tax, and banking separation clean. If multiple locations sit inside one legal entity, you can track those units with classes inside that entity's single file. Files follow legal entities; classes distinguish units within an entity.

mainEntity.name: Should franchise bookkeeping use classes or locations in QuickBooks Online?

acceptedAnswer.text: Within a single entity's file, use Location tracking for the unit itself (one location per transaction, cleaner for unit P&Ls) and reserve Classes for a second dimension like department or revenue channel. Never use either feature to simulate separate legal entities; that's what separate company files are for.

mainEntity.name: How do I get a consolidated P&L across multiple QuickBooks Online files?

acceptedAnswer.text: QuickBooks Online has no native multi-file consolidation. Options are a manual Excel combine each month (export each P&L, align accounts, sum), which gets fragile past three units, or a consolidation platform like FinBoard.ai that syncs every file, maps each chart of accounts to one standard, applies eliminations and allocations, and produces unit-level and consolidated statements automatically.

mainEntity.name: How do franchisor royalty reports affect the classes-vs-files decision?

acceptedAnswer.text: Royalties and ad-fund fees are calculated per unit on defined gross sales, and franchisors typically mandate a unit-level P&L format and reserve audit rights. Separate files make the per-unit revenue base unambiguous and auditable; a shared file makes it dependent on perfect class tagging, where one untagged deposit misstates the royalty base. Sales-based fees plus audit rights weigh heavily toward file-per-entity with a consolidation layer.

content:
The Question Every Multi-Unit Owner Hits at Unit Two

Your first franchise location was simple. One QuickBooks Online file, one bank account, one P&L. Then you signed the lease on unit two, and suddenly you're staring at a fork in the road that will shape your accounting for the next decade: do you add the new location to your existing QuickBooks file and track it with classes or locations, or do you set up a separate QuickBooks company for each franchise location?

Most owners make this decision in about ten minutes, usually based on whatever their bookkeeper is comfortable with. That's a mistake. The choice determines how you calculate royalties, how you report to your franchisor, whether your lender gets clean unit-level financials, and how painful (or automatic) your consolidated P&L will be when you're running eight units instead of two.

Both options are laid out below, warts included, along with a decision framework you can actually apply and the hybrid model most successful multi-unit franchise groups eventually land on.

Option A: One QuickBooks File with Classes or Locations

In this model, every unit lives inside a single QuickBooks Online company file. You use the Class or Location tracking feature (available on QBO Plus and Advanced) to tag every transaction with the unit it belongs to. Run a P&L by Class, and you get columns for each location side by side.

Where classes shine

  • Instant unit comparison. P&L by Class puts Unit 1 next to Unit 2 next to Unit 3 in one report, with a total column. No exports, no spreadsheets.
  • One subscription, one login. You pay for a single QBO Plus or Advanced subscription instead of one per unit. One chart of accounts, one vendor list, one payroll integration.
  • Shared vendors and bills are trivial. A food distributor invoice covering three units can be split across classes on a single bill.
  • Simpler month-end close. One bank feed review, one reconciliation cycle, one close checklist.

Where classes break

  • Classes cannot represent separate legal entities. This is the big one. If each unit is its own LLC (the standard structure franchise attorneys recommend for liability isolation), then a single QuickBooks file is commingling the books of legally distinct companies. Your balance sheet becomes a blended mess: whose cash is whose? Which LLC owes the credit card balance? An attorney trying to defend your corporate veil, or an accountant preparing separate tax returns, will not thank you.
  • Untagged transactions pollute everything. Every transaction must be classed manually or by rule. In practice, 5 to 10% slip through untagged, and your "Not Specified" column quietly absorbs real revenue and costs that belong to specific units. Royalty calculations built on classed revenue are now wrong.
  • Balance sheet by class is unreliable. QuickBooks Online can filter a balance sheet by class, but many balance sheet transactions (transfers, loan payments, equity moves) don't carry classes cleanly. You'll get unit P&Ls that look fine and balance sheets that don't balance by unit.
  • The audit trail gets messy across entities. When one file contains the activity of multiple LLCs, every journal entry, every correction, every bank transfer between units is tangled into one ledger. Untangling it later for a sale, a partner dispute, or a franchisor audit is forensic work.
  • Separate banking fights the model. If each unit has its own bank account (it should), you're reconciling multiple bank feeds inside one file and constantly checking that transfers between accounts were classed as intercompany moves, not income.

Option B: One QuickBooks Company File Per Unit

Here, every franchise location gets its own QuickBooks Online subscription. Each file has its own chart of accounts, its own bank feeds, its own balance sheet, its own audit trail.

Where separate files shine

  • Clean legal separation. One LLC, one file, one set of books. Tax returns are straightforward. The corporate veil stays intact. If you sell a single unit, its books transfer cleanly with it.
  • Real balance sheets per unit. Cash, loans, fixed assets, and equity are tracked properly per entity, which is something classes never quite deliver.
  • Unit-level royalty and franchisor reporting is native. Gross sales for royalty and ad-fund calculations come straight off each unit's P&L, with no risk of another unit's untagged revenue leaking in.
  • Lender and landlord reporting just works. An SBA lender financing unit 4, or a landlord requiring unit-level financials for a percentage-rent lease, gets a standalone statement without surgery.
  • Independent audit trails. Each entity's history is self-contained. Franchisor audits, due diligence, and disputes touch only the file in question.

Where separate files break

  • There is no native consolidated P&L. QuickBooks Online has no built-in way to combine multiple company files into one report. Your "consolidated" view is a monthly Excel exercise: export each P&L, paste into a workbook, align the rows, pray nobody renamed an account. At three units it's annoying; at eight it's someone's part-time job.
  • Charts of accounts drift. File three adds a "Delivery Fees" account that files one and two don't have. Six months later, no two P&Ls line up row-for-row, and every consolidation starts with manual re-mapping.
  • Shared costs need allocation machinery. Corporate office salaries, group marketing, and your own management fee all hit one entity's books and must be allocated or recharged to units via intercompany entries you build and maintain yourself.
  • Cost and admin multiply. Each file is a separate QBO subscription, separate bank feed setup, separate close. Five units means five closes.
  • Intercompany activity needs discipline. Transfers, shared purchases, and management fees between entities create intercompany balances that must be tracked and eliminated in any combined view. See our practical guide to intercompany eliminations.

The Decision Framework

Here's the test we'd apply to any multi-unit franchise group. Go criterion by criterion; the verdicts compound quickly.

CriterionOne File + ClassesFile Per UnitVerdict
Separate legal entities?Commingles books of distinct LLCs; weakens liability protection and complicates tax returnsOne entity, one ledger, with clean legal and tax separationIf each unit is its own LLC, this alone forces separate files. Non-negotiable.
Separate bank accounts per unit?Multiple feeds in one file; inter-account transfers constantly misclassifiedEach file owns its own bank feeds and reconciliationsSeparate banking strongly favors separate files
Franchisor reporting requirementsUnit P&L possible, but untagged transactions corrupt the mandated formatNative unit-level P&L; mapping to the franchisor's format still neededSeparate files if the franchisor mandates a unit-level P&L format (most do)
Royalty & ad-fund calculationDepends on perfect class discipline on every sales transactionGross sales per unit come straight off each entity's booksSeparate files. Royalties computed on leaky class data invite franchisor disputes
Lender / landlord reportingFiltered statements per class; balance sheet by class rarely holds upStandalone financials per entity, balance sheet includedSeparate files if any unit has its own loan or percentage-rent lease
Unit countWorkable at 2 to 3 units under one entity; class lists get unwieldy beyond thatScales structurally, but manual consolidation pain grows with each fileUnder 3 units + single entity: classes can work. 4+: separate files + tooling
Consolidation needBuilt-in: P&L by Class with a total columnNone native; manual Excel combine or a consolidation layer on topThe one criterion favoring classes, unless you add a consolidation layer

Read the table honestly and a pattern emerges: the only row where classes clearly win is consolidation convenience. Every row about legal structure, banking, franchisor compliance, royalties, and third-party reporting points at separate files. That's why the real question shifts from "classes or files?" to "how do I get consolidation back after choosing files?"

The Hybrid Reality: File Per Entity, Plus a Consolidation Layer

In practice, most multi-unit franchise groups converge on the same architecture, usually after a painful year or two of fighting one of the pure models:

  • One QuickBooks Online file per legal entity (often, but not always, one entity per unit; some owners hold two or three units in one LLC and use classes within that file for the units it contains).
  • A consolidation layer on top that maps every file to a standard chart of accounts and produces unit-level and combined reporting automatically.

This hybrid gets you the legal cleanliness, banking separation, and franchisor-ready unit books of Option B, without inheriting its worst flaw, the monthly Excel consolidation ritual. The classes-within-a-file trick still has a place, but only inside a single legal entity, never across entities.

What the Consolidation Layer Must Actually Do

Not every "multi-company reporting" tool clears the bar for franchise work. Four capabilities are load-bearing:

1. Standard chart of accounts mapping. Each unit's file will drift: different bookkeepers, different account names, one-off additions. The layer must map every local account to one group-standard chart (ideally the franchisor's mandated format), so "Food Cost" in unit 2 and "COGS - Food" in unit 5 roll up to the same line, every month, without re-doing the mapping.

2. Unit-level and consolidated P&L from the same data. You need the side-by-side unit comparison classes used to give you and the combined group view, driven by one set of numbers so they always reconcile. If your restaurant units track prime cost, this is where location-level prime cost reporting lives too.

3. Royalty-ready reports. Royalties and ad-fund contributions are calculated per unit on defined revenue lines. The layer should produce a per-unit gross sales report in the franchisor's format on schedule, traceable back to each entity's ledger, so a franchisor audit is a link, not a scramble.

4. Shared-cost allocations and eliminations. Corporate payroll, group marketing, and management fees need rule-based allocation across units (by revenue, by headcount, or flat), and intercompany charges need eliminating in the consolidated view so group revenue isn't inflated by money moving between your own entities. These are exactly the multi-entity consolidation gaps that spreadsheets handle worst.

How FinBoard.ai Handles Franchise Consolidation

FinBoard.ai is built for exactly this architecture: multiple QuickBooks Online files, one governed reporting layer.

  • Connect every unit's QBO file and sync transaction-level data continuously, with no monthly exports.
  • Map each local chart of accounts once to a standard group chart; AI-assisted mapping handles the drift as bookkeepers add accounts.
  • Get unit-level and consolidated P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow from the same underlying ledger data, with drill-down from any consolidated number to the source transactions in the source file.
  • Define royalty and ad-fund revenue bases once and report them per unit, every period, in a consistent format.
  • Apply allocation rules and intercompany eliminations so shared costs land where they belong and the group P&L reflects real outside revenue.

The result is the hybrid model without the manual glue: each LLC keeps clean standalone books, and you get the comparison and consolidation classes promised but never quite delivered. See how FinBoard.ai consolidates multi-unit franchise books →

Migrating from Classes to Separate Entities

If you started with everything in one file and now need to split (usually triggered by forming new LLCs, taking on unit-level debt, or a franchisor compliance push), the path looks like this:

  1. Pick a clean cutover date. A fiscal year start is ideal; a quarter start is acceptable. Don't split mid-month.
  2. Stand up one new QBO file per entity using the group-standard chart of accounts from day one. This is your chance to fix drift permanently.
  3. Enter opening balances per entity from a class-filtered trial balance, and expect to make judgment calls: unclassed balance sheet items must be assigned to an entity deliberately, with documentation.
  4. Point banking, payroll, and POS integrations at the correct new file per unit.
  5. Keep the old file read-only as the historical archive. Don't delete it; it's your audit trail for everything before the cutover.
  6. Connect the new files to your consolidation layer immediately, so comparative reporting survives the transition instead of restarting from zero.

Budget four to six weeks of parallel attention for a clean split. It's real work, which is one more argument for choosing the file-per-entity structure at unit two, when migration costs are near zero.

FAQ

Do I need a separate QuickBooks company for each franchise location?

If each location is a separate legal entity (its own LLC or corporation), yes. Each entity needs its own QuickBooks company file to keep legal, tax, and banking separation clean. If multiple locations sit inside one legal entity, you can track those specific units with classes inside that entity's single file. The rule is: files follow legal entities; classes distinguish units within an entity.

Should franchise bookkeeping use classes or locations in QuickBooks Online?

Within a single entity's file, use Location tracking for the unit itself (one location per transaction, cleaner for unit P&Ls) and reserve Classes for a second dimension like department or revenue channel if you need one. Never use either feature to simulate separate legal entities; that's what separate company files are for.

How do I get a consolidated P&L across multiple QuickBooks Online files?

QuickBooks Online has no native multi-file consolidation. Your options are a manual Excel combine each month (export each P&L, align accounts, sum), which gets fragile and slow past three units, or a consolidation platform like FinBoard.ai that syncs every file, maps each chart of accounts to one standard, applies eliminations and allocations, and produces unit-level and consolidated statements automatically.

How do franchisor royalty reports affect the classes-vs-files decision?

Royalties and ad-fund fees are calculated per unit on defined gross sales, and franchisors typically mandate a unit-level P&L format and reserve audit rights. Separate files make the per-unit revenue base unambiguous and auditable; a shared file makes it dependent on perfect class tagging, where one untagged deposit misstates the royalty base. If your franchise agreement includes sales-based fees and audit rights, that weighs heavily toward file-per-entity with a consolidation layer on top.
